channel.title: Demo Dispatch
channel.description: A sample site for previewing WordPress themes.

wp:term.wp:term_description: The studio journal. Short news, releases, openings, and announcements from the four members. Multi-author. Flatters news and magazine themes.
The long pieces. Editorial essays on objects, places, and the slow work of paying attention. Flatters blog and journal themes.
Case studies of design and editorial projects the studio has shipped for clients. Flatters portfolio and agency themes.

Parent category for everything the studio puts a label on — Shop Shelf sits under here. Demonstrates nested category permalinks (/category/catalog/shop-shelf/) and the breadcrumb behaviour many themes layer onto wp:post-terms.
Product-shaped posts for items the studio sells in its small shop — zines, prints, notebooks, tea blends. Flatters shop-adjacent themes.
Notes from the small kitchen at the front of the studio — what's on this week, where the ingredients came from, the occasional recipe fragment. Flatters food and restaurant themes.
Theme stress tests. Reachable from /category/sandbox/. Below the home page fold by date discipline. Verifies edge cases without polluting the public feed.

item.title: How a lighthouse works
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

A lighthouse, when you stop to think about it, is really a clock. It tells sailors not just where they are but, more usefully, which light they are looking at. Every lighthouse on every coast in the world has a unique signature - a pattern of flashes and pauses, sometimes broken by a longer beam, sometimes a steady glow. Mariners learn the patterns the way they once learned the shapes of constellations. From a small boat at four in the morning, in the kind of weather where the sky and the sea are the same colour, a lighthouse identifies itself in something close to Morse.

The Fresnel question

For most of human history, putting a useful light on top of a tower was a very inefficient business. A bonfire on a rock can be seen for a few miles in clear weather, but a few miles is not enough. Oil lamps with reflectors did better, but the reflector wastes most of the light by sending it back through the lamp itself. The breakthrough was a French civil engineer named Augustin Fresnel, who realised in the 1820s that a lens did not need to be one solid block of glass. You could carve concentric rings out of a thicker lens, discard the bulk between them, and end up with a flat-ish disk that bent light just as effectively as the original. Stack a series of these rings into a beehive and you had something that could take a single oil flame and throw it twenty miles.

A first-order Fresnel lens stands taller than a person. It looks, in photographs, like a mechanical hive built out of glass and brass. It is heavy. The largest of them weighed more than a small car. Once a lens like this was installed in a tower, it tended to stay installed - many of them have been in continuous service for more than a century, occasionally disassembled, cleaned, and reassembled in exactly the configuration the keeper found them.

The flash pattern

Here is the part that surprised me when I first learned it. The light inside a lighthouse is, in most cases, not blinking. It is on, continuously, like a bedside lamp. What blinks is the lens. The whole assembly of brass and glass floats on a mercury bath - a shallow trough of liquid metal that lets the several-tonne lens turn with almost no friction - and a clockwork mechanism, wound by hand once or twice a day, drives it slowly around. The "flash" you see from the sea is the moment when one of the lens's segments is pointed directly at you. The "dark" is when it is pointed somewhere else.

The pattern of flashes is the lighthouse's name in the language of charts. Three quick flashes, four seconds dark, repeat: that is one lighthouse. Single flash every ten seconds: that is another. A "group flash four" might be one lighthouse on the western approach to a strait; a "group flash three white, one red" might be the next one up the coast. Open any nautical chart and the lighthouses are listed by these signatures, the way other places are listed by latitude and longitude.

This means the lighthouse is doing two things at once. It is being a light

  • a thing visible at a great distance, in foul weather, when no other

landmark is. And it is being a clock - a mechanism so reliable that a sailor can pull out a stopwatch, count the flashes, and know with certainty which piece of coast the light belongs to.

Keepers

Until quite recently, every lighthouse had at least one keeper - usually two or three, working in shifts. The job was, by most accounts, both extremely boring and extremely demanding. The keeper wound the clockwork. The keeper filled the lamp. The keeper polished the brass and the glass, every day, because soot accumulated and obscured the beam. The keeper kept the log, which recorded the weather and the passing ships and any unusual events. The keeper, in storms, often saved lives.

There is a romance about lighthouse keepers that is mostly imaginary. Most of them were not solitary poets. Most of them were practical people doing a job that had to be done, on rocks they did not always like, alongside colleagues they did not always like either. The logs are full of disagreements about who was supposed to fill the oil reservoir on Tuesday. But the romance has stuck, partly because of nineteenth-century novels and partly because the work itself - keeping a single light burning through the night, on a rock, for nobody in particular - is a useful image of what it means to do something carefully.

Automation

Almost every lighthouse in the world is now automated. The lamps are electric, often LED. The lenses, in many cases, are smaller and lighter than the historic Fresnels, which have been removed and put in maritime museums. The flash patterns are produced by computers rather than by the slow revolution of clockwork. The keepers are gone. A single technician now visits a string of lighthouses on a rotation, sometimes by helicopter, checking solar panels and replacing batteries.

The lights themselves are still doing the same job. They identify themselves to sailors, in the same coded patterns, on the same nautical charts. They are still, in a real and useful sense, clocks. But they no longer require anyone to wind them.

I find this both reassuring and slightly sad. A lighthouse is, after all, a machine designed to be paid attention to. It is built to be seen by someone who is alone at night and worried. The fact that nobody now climbs the spiral stairs to wind the clockwork - that the thing runs itself, on a solar panel, talking to a satellite - is an engineering triumph. It is also a kind of loneliness on the lighthouse's behalf.

The next time you find yourself near a coast at dusk, watch for the lights to come on. They will, in a sequence you cannot quite predict, all begin flashing at once, each in its own pattern. Somewhere out at sea, a sailor will be checking them off. It is, when you think about it, a very old conversation.
item.excerpt:encoded: A long-form essay covering Fresnel lenses, characteristic flash patterns, and why a lighthouse is really a clock.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I grew up two streets away from a working lighthouse. The flash pattern is, even now, the rhythm I fall asleep to when I am anywhere coastal. Beautiful piece.
Thank you, Eleni. Which lighthouse, if you don't mind sharing?
A small one on a headland - characteristic flash four white every twenty seconds. I won't say which exactly. The headland is small and quiet and I would like to keep it that way.
For anyone wanting to dig into Fresnel lens construction, here is a useful (and very long) URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fresnel_lens#Construction_and_optical_principles_of_lighthouse_lenses
Re: the mercury bath - I had no idea. @tomas is there a good book on lighthouse mechanisms you would recommend?
Thora Williams's *Towers of Light* is excellent if a little technical. Bella Bathurst's *The Lighthouse Stevensons* is more narrative. I have not read anything as good in the last decade.
日本にも同じような灯台があります。This piece reminded me of the small light at the entrance to my grandmother's harbour. Thank you.
[...] this lovely essay on how a lighthouse works [...]
[...] which is to say, a lighthouse is really a clock [...]

For the curious: a one-liner that prints the next ten flashes of a "group flash four every twenty seconds" lighthouse.
```python for n in range(10): print(f"flash at t={n*20}s") ```
This comment is in pending-moderation status so theme reviewers can confirm the active theme styles awaiting-moderation comments distinguishably from approved ones.

item.title: Spring show, now open
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
The spring show is up in the front gallery. Six pieces, each numbered and priced, hung against the new sage wall the way Daniyal kept arguing for and the rest of us kept resisting. He was right. They look better there.

The work is by three people we have wanted to show for a year. Two prints, two small oils, and two pieces in mixed media that I do not know what to call. Come and tell me what you think.

The show runs through June 14. The studio is open Wednesday through Sunday, ten until six, with the gallery accessible during all open hours. The opening reception is this Friday evening, six until eight, with bread from the bakery and the house tea from the kitchen.

A short walk-through is on the Visit page, including how to get here from the harbor. If you want to write about it, the Press kit has the artists' bios and three usage-cleared photographs.
item.excerpt:encoded: A short Dispatch announcing the spring exhibition opening in the front gallery, hours, and a brief note on the work.

item.title: A new mark for Bell Press
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
Client: Bell Press, a 70-year-old neighborhood print shop two streets over.

Brief: A new wordmark, a small identity system, and a single two-color letterpress card to announce the move to the new shopfront. Quietly modern. No "rebrand" in the sense the word usually means. The old hand-painted sign on the front window had to feel related, not replaced.

Approach. We spent a morning at the press with Bell himself, looking at sixty years of his own composing-stick work. The wordmark we eventually drew borrows the long descenders and the wide counter from his 1972 wedding invitations and nothing else.

The card is two passes — a warm grey for the address block, a deep terracotta for the wordmark. We proofed it twice and printed a hundred for the opening.

Outcome. Bell hung the new sign on the new shopfront last Tuesday. The mark looks like it has always been there, which is what Bell asked for and what we hoped to do.

A small case study, by design. Print is in the Shop for collectors who want one of the trial posters. Larger work for Bell is ongoing.
item.excerpt:encoded: Identity, two-color letterpress card, and a small wordmark for a 70- year-old neighborhood print shop. Brief / approach / outcome.

item.title: Lemon cake, Thursday
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
We are making lemon cake again on Thursday. The lemons are from the same small grove they were in February, brought up by the woman with the green truck who is on her way to a market further east and stops at the studio on the way past.

This time we are using a little less sugar and a little more zest, which fixes a thing that was bothering me about the last batch — it was sweet but not very lemon, which seems like the wrong direction for a cake that has "lemon" in the name.

The recipe is the one in the back of Quarterly, the small cookbook we made for a friend last winter. It will be on the Menu Thursday and Friday, sold by the slice, until it is gone. It goes well with the house tea.

If you want to know when the next one is, Tomás keeps a short list and will write you when there is more.
item.excerpt:encoded: Notes on this Thursday's lemon cake: the lemons, the proportions, and what we changed from last time.

item.title: Shop: Quiet Rooms, a 24-page zine
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
A small two-color zine, 24 pages, collecting six short essays from the Field Notes archive about rooms and the quiet inside them. Letterpress covers, offset interior, sewn binding.

What's in it

  • Six essays, lightly edited from their original Dispatch versions.
  • Three new line drawings.
  • An afterword about the studio's north-facing room and why it is the way

it is.

Specifications

  • 24 pages, A5, perfect-bound.
  • Cover: 220gsm Colorplan in terracotta, two-pass letterpress.
  • Interior: 100gsm uncoated cream, offset.
  • Run of 200, numbered.

Price: $18.

Availability: In stock. Ships in a flat envelope, US and international. Allow 7–10 days to ship from the studio.

If you want one, the Shop page has the order form, or come pick it up at the front desk during open hours. There is a stack on the counter.
item.excerpt:encoded: A two-color zine collecting six short essays from Field Notes, printed letterpress in a run of 200. Includes price, contents, and availability.

item.title: Notes on the color blue
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

Blue, as a colour, has had a very strange career. It is the colour of the sky, which is the largest object most of us see in any given day, and it is the colour of the sea, which is the second largest. And yet, for most of human history, it has been almost impossible to make.

The first useful blue pigment in the western world was lapis lazuli, ground from a stone mined almost exclusively in what is now Afghanistan. By the time it reached a European studio in the fifteenth century, it cost more by weight than gold. Painters used it sparingly, sometimes negotiating with their patrons over how many square inches of robe could be that particular, extravagant ultramarine. Mary's robes, in many altarpieces, are blue not because someone decided blue was the right colour for Mary but because blue was the most expensive thing the workshop could afford to put on the panel, and Mary deserved it.

A long absence

Languages, on the whole, did not have a word for blue until quite late in their development. Homer described the sea as "wine-dark." Ancient Hebrew, ancient Chinese, ancient Japanese all routinely grouped together what we would now call blue and green, or blue and grey, or sometimes blue and black. This is not because their speakers could not see the colour. It is because the colour was not, for most purposes, distinct enough from its neighbours to need its own word. You named the colours that you needed to name. You needed to name red, because red was blood and red was warning. You did not need to name blue, because nothing useful in your immediate environment was decisively, only blue.

The Egyptians were an exception. They synthesised a blue pigment from copper, sand, and lime - the famous Egyptian blue - and used it everywhere. Their tomb paintings glow with it. The technique was lost after Rome fell, and Europe spent more than a thousand years going back to grinding stones.

Industrial blue

The breakthrough was, as is often the case, an accident. In 1704 a German chemist named Heinrich Diesbach was trying to make a red pigment. He failed. What he made instead was a deep, slightly purple blue, almost as good as ultramarine and almost as cheap as dirt. Prussian blue, as it came to be called, made blue available to ordinary printers and ordinary painters for the first time.

Hokusai used it. The great wave is Prussian blue. Vermeer's late paintings are full of it. The blueprint, that nineteenth-century technical drawing that copied a master onto blue-tinted paper, is Prussian blue. Once you can make a blue cheaply, blue stops being a luxury and becomes a default. By the 1860s, blue was, for the first time in human history, ordinary.

Klein

In 1956 a French painter named Yves Klein started making paintings that were nothing but blue. One canvas. One colour. He called the particular shade he used International Klein Blue, and he eventually patented it, though the patent covered the binder rather than the pigment itself. The pigment was a synthetic ultramarine that had been available for decades. Klein's contribution was insisting that you look at the colour without anything else to look at.

It is, I think, the right way to end a brief history of blue. For thousands of years humans worked very hard to put a small amount of blue on something else - on a wall, in a manuscript, on a panel of a saint. And then eventually somebody made a painting that was just blue. There was nothing else in the frame. The work was the colour.

I think about this when I open a fresh bottle of ink. The ink is, almost always, a particular shade of blue-black. It is cheaper than I can imagine. It pours the same as it would have poured in any century. And the shade inside the bottle is darker, deeper, and more interesting than any single material the western world had access to before about 1700.

It is, every time, slightly miraculous.
item.excerpt:encoded: An essay on the long, strange history of blue as a pigment, ending at the Yves Klein patent.

item.title: Workshop: Letterpress, Saturday afternoon
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
Daniyal is running a half-day letterpress workshop on Saturday, the 12th of April, from one in the afternoon until five.

Six places, beginners welcome. You will set type by hand, lock up a small forme, ink the bed, and pull a short run of two-color cards on the proofing press. By the end of the afternoon you will have ten cards you printed yourself and a much better understanding of why your favorite typefaces look the way they do.

What to bring

  • Comfortable clothes you do not mind getting ink on.
  • A small notebook, if you keep one.
  • Lunch is not provided but the kitchen is open, and the bread is good.

Fee: $80, which covers materials and use of the press. We waive the fee for one place per workshop, no questions asked — write to Tomás if that is useful.

To reserve a place, write to the studio via the Contact page. First six replies, in order received.
item.excerpt:encoded: Announcement for the half-day letterpress workshop, with what to bring and the fee.

item.title: Morning bread, from the bakery down the block
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
People ask, often enough that it is worth writing down: no, we do not bake the bread ourselves. The bread comes from the bakery down the block, the one with the blue door, run by a couple who get up earlier than I do and who have been at it for nineteen years.

We tried baking it in the early days. It was fine. It was not as good as the bread from the bakery down the block, and it tied up the small oven for three hours every morning, and the oven is the only piece of equipment in the kitchen we have a real argument with. So we decided we would rather pay for good bread than make mediocre bread.

The loaves come over at six in the morning, four to start, more on Saturdays. They are on the counter by seven. If you come early they are still warm. Sliced and toasted with butter, with the lemon marmalade we put up in February, with anything from the Menu — they are the quiet center of every morning here.

If you ever want to know whose bread you are eating, walk one block south and look for the blue door. Tell them we sent you.
item.excerpt:encoded: A short post about where the morning bread comes from and why we don't bake it ourselves.

item.title: A short history of the printed page
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

Before the printed page there was the manuscript page, and before the manuscript page there was the scroll, and before the scroll there was the clay tablet. Each of these is, at root, the same problem with a different solution: how do you store words such that another person, somewhere else, can read them later.

Each solution constrains what the words can do. The clay tablet is good for short, durable, official communication - tax records, legal codes, the kind of message that benefits from being literally set in stone. It is bad for a long argument. The scroll is better for a long argument but terrible for finding a specific passage; you have to roll the whole thing forward until you arrive at it. The codex - what we would now just call a book, with a spine and pages you turn - is the format that makes random access practical, which is why it became, and remains, the dominant form.

Type before the press

The cleverness of moveable type, when it arrived in Europe in the 1450s, was not the type itself. The Chinese had been using moveable type for four centuries. The cleverness was the alloy. Gutenberg's contribution was a specific, repeatable mixture of lead, tin, and antimony that cooled with exactly the right combination of properties: it was hard enough to take an inked impression thousands of times without deforming, soft enough to cast into a precise mould, and shrunk in a predictable way as it cooled, so the finished letter was the size you intended.

Without that alloy, moveable type was a craft project. With it, you had an industry.

Paper sizes

The standard paper sizes we now use - A4, A3, A5, and so on - were formalised in Germany in the 1920s but the principle is much older. They are all related by a single ratio: the long side of any sheet, divided by the short side, equals the square root of two. This means a sheet folded in half yields two sheets that are exactly the same proportion as the original. You can cut paper without waste. You can scale a layout from one size to another without distorting it.

It is, when you stop to look, a beautiful piece of design. It is also one of the only pieces of standardised infrastructure that the United States declined to adopt - American paper sizes are unrelated to any clean mathematical proportion and require their own separate scaling tables in every piece of design software.

After the press

For five hundred years after Gutenberg, the page was set in metal type. The typesetter stood at a case of small lead letters, picking them out one at a time, assembling each line by hand into a metal frame. The frame was inked, pressed against paper, and the page was printed. To change a typo, you unlocked the frame, picked out the wrong letter, found the right one, and locked the frame again.

In the 1880s a machine called the Linotype let an operator type the words on a keyboard and got the machine to cast a fresh slug of type for each line. This was much faster but it meant that to fix a typo you had to recast the entire line. Newspapers ran on Linotype machines well into the 1980s.

In the 1960s phototypesetting did away with metal entirely. Letters were exposed onto film and the film was then used to make printing plates. By the late 1980s, phototypesetting had in turn been replaced by PostScript-driven imagesetters, which were eventually replaced by direct-to-plate systems, which now coexist with the entirely paperless workflows of digital publishing.

Each of these transitions was experienced, by the people doing the work, as the loss of a craft. Each of them was, on net, an improvement.

What the page is now

The page is now mostly virtual. You are reading this on a screen that does not have pages. The line you are reading does not have a fixed length, a fixed typeface, or a fixed colour; the rendering changes if you resize your window. The conventions of the printed page - the title at the top, the paragraph indent, the line spacing - are still here, but they are suggestions now rather than constraints. The page itself has been disassembled into a stream of text and a set of style rules, and reassembled, in front of you, in real time.

This is, in its way, another revolution comparable to Gutenberg's. We have not yet finished working out what it is for. We are, mostly, still using the new medium to do what the old medium did. Paragraphs. Headings. Blockquotes. Numbered lists.

The clay tablets are still legible four thousand years later. Whether the glowing rectangles will be remembered as a stage in the evolution of the page, or as the format that finally retired it, is something nobody alive today is in a position to know.
item.excerpt:encoded: From woodblock to movable type to phototypesetting, with sidebars on paper sizes.

item.title: Wayfinding for the harbor museum
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
Client: A one-room museum at the end of the breakwater. Forty exhibits, one volunteer at the door, no signage budget for fifteen years.

Brief: A small, durable wayfinding system that fits the space. Cards visitors can carry, a single exterior sign, and a folded plan of the room. Read at a glance by anyone walking in cold.

Approach. We spent a Saturday in the museum watching how people moved through it. They moved badly, the way people do in any one-room museum, in loops, missing the case in the far corner with the brass log every time.

We made three things.

First, a folded plan of the room, A5 closed and A2 open, with the forty exhibits numbered in the order the volunteers recommend you see them. One edge of the plan is a small narrative — the history of the breakwater in six paragraphs.

Second, a deck of forty pocket cards, one per exhibit, in two colors. The front of each card is the exhibit's name, number, and date. The back is a single paragraph that tells you what to look for. The cards live in a small wooden tray near the door.

Third, an exterior sign. Cast aluminum, two-color enamel, mounted at eye height on the seaward side of the building so it catches the light from the afternoon. It says simply: HARBOR MUSEUM, OPEN.

Outcome. Visits up forty percent in the first month, according to the volunteer at the door, who is keeping a tally on a postcard taped above the cash box. People are now finding the brass log in the corner.
item.excerpt:encoded: A small wayfinding system for a one-room museum: floor plan, deck of cards, exterior sign. Brief / approach / outcome.

item.title: Shop: Harbor at five o'clock, two-color print
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
A two-color screenprint of the harbor at five o'clock in the afternoon, when the light comes off the water at the angle Tomás keeps writing about. Pulled in the studio's print room one Saturday in February.

What's in it

  • A single screenprint, two passes, terracotta and ocean teal, on a

cream cotton paper.

  • Numbered, signed in pencil along the lower edge.
  • Comes flat in a board-stiffened envelope.

Specifications

  • Image area: 30 × 40 cm.
  • Paper: 250gsm Somerset, cream.
  • Edition: 50, numbered 1/50 to 50/50.

Price: $45.

Availability: 32 of 50 still available at time of writing. Once the edition is gone, it will not be reprinted.

To order, write via the Contact page or come pick one up at the front desk during open hours. International shipping is $14 in a flat mailer.
item.excerpt:encoded: A two-color screenprint of the harbor at five o'clock. Numbered edition of 50. Includes price, dimensions, and shipping note.

item.title: Issue three is now shipping
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
The third issue is back from the printer and shipping starts this week. If you pre-ordered, your copy is in the first batch and should arrive within the next ten days. If you did not, the Shop has copies, while they last.

This issue is 48 pages, perfect-bound, with a lithograph cover by an artist we admire and three new pieces of writing — one from Mira on the long blue of February evenings, a project case study from Daniyal, and a recipe from Akosua that involves a quince and a great deal of patience. We are pleased with how it turned out.

There is also a small index at the back of every previous issue's contents, which several readers asked for. It does not look like much. We hope it is useful.

Thanks for reading.
item.excerpt:encoded: Announcement that Issue three has gone to press and shipping starts this week.

item.title: Why we still write postcards
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

A postcard is the slowest message worth sending. There are slower messages - a letter is slower, a telegram, in the few corners of the world where it still exists, would be even slower if it were not also extinct - but the postcard is the slowest message that anyone now routinely sends. You write it sitting at a small café table, you carry it in your bag for a day or two, you find a postbox, you drop it in. Two weeks later it arrives in somebody else's letterbox. By then, you are home.

This is the entire point.

A text message arrives instantly and conveys, on average, almost nothing. "Hi." "Wish you were here." "Got home safe." A postcard arrives slowly and also conveys almost nothing - the same "wish you were here," the same weather report - but the slowness changes the meaning. A postcard is not a message. A postcard is a small physical object that someone you know selected, carried, wrote on, addressed, and posted, on a specific day, in a specific place. The text is almost beside the point. The text is what is written on the back of the gift.

Cards as objects

I keep all my postcards. There are not many - perhaps two or three a year arrive at my flat - but each one stays. They live in a small wooden box on the mantelpiece, in no particular order, and once or twice a year I take them out and read them and put them back.

The text on most of the cards is unmemorable. A friend's holiday in Norfolk. Another friend's bad weather in the Lake District. A half-illegible note from someone I have not seen in three years. The point of the box is not the text. The point of the box is that all of these people, on a specific morning, in a specific town, decided I was worth a small piece of paper and a postage stamp.

What gets lost in the post

Royal Mail loses a small percentage of postcards. So does every other postal service. There is no return address on most postcards, so a lost postcard is not returned to the sender; it is, eventually, destroyed.

I find this enormously moving. Somebody chose the card, paid for the card, wrote on the card, addressed it, posted it, and then it never arrived. The recipient does not know, because they never expected it. The sender, after a few months, suspects, but cannot be sure - perhaps the friend just hasn't mentioned it. The card sits in a sorting office somewhere, then in a bin, then in landfill. The kind thought it represented exists, has always existed, but is now invisible to everyone except the person who had it.

Almost every kind thought, in the entire history of the world, has been like this. The postcard makes the everyday case visible.

Buy six and write them all today

If you are travelling: buy six postcards from the first newsagent you find. Write them, all of them, that evening. Address them. Post them in the morning. You will pay slightly less than you would for one of those small, disappointing meals you do not really want anyway. The cards will arrive, weeks later, scattered across whatever week your friends are having - none of them will arrive on the same day. Each of them will be small, and brief, and not quite enough.

That is, I think, exactly what they are for.
item.excerpt:encoded: A defense of the postcard as the slowest message worth sending.

item.title: Quarterly: a 96-page cookbook for a friend
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
Client: A friend who runs a small restaurant two cities east, and her mother, who has been writing the recipes down since 1971.

Brief: A small-press cookbook collecting forty of her mother's recipes. Hand-set headings, generous margins, full-bleed photographs of the food. A book the friend would want to give to her own daughter.

Approach. We worked through the recipes one weekend at a long kitchen table, deciding which forty out of the seventy-three the mother had written. We cooked twelve of them ourselves before we had any opinions about how the book should look.

The book is set in a single typeface family, contemporary, with old-style numerals for the quantities. Headings are hand-set on the studio's press in a contrasting display face and pasted into the master at the right angle to look as though they were typed onto each page. The mother liked this very much.

Outcome. Ninety-six pages, sewn binding, hard cover with a blind-debossed spine. A run of three hundred. The friend gave the first copy to her mother on a Tuesday afternoon and called us afterwards. The mother had read it straight through, looking for what was missing. She said nothing was.

A few copies of the second printing are on the Shop page when they exist. The first printing is gone.
item.excerpt:encoded: Editorial design and typesetting for a small-press cookbook. Includes a five-image gallery of spreads.

item.title: Lemon ice
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

A two-ingredient recipe. Best made the day before you want to eat it.

Ingredients

  • 6 lemons (unwaxed if you can find them).
  • 200g caster sugar (white).
  • 600ml water.

The sugar and the water make a syrup. The lemons make the rest of it.

Method

  1. Zest two of the lemons. Set the zest aside.
  2. Juice all six lemons. You should end up with about 250ml of juice.
  3. Combine the sugar and water in a saucepan. Bring to a simmer, stirring

only until the sugar dissolves. Add the zest. Take off the heat.

  1. Let the syrup cool, then strain out the zest.
  2. Stir in the lemon juice.
  3. Pour the mixture into a shallow tray and put it in the freezer.
  4. After 90 minutes, take it out and rake it through with a fork. Return

to the freezer. Repeat every 90 minutes for the next four hours, or until the texture is the granular, snow-like consistency you want.

Serve in small, cold cups.

It is the right pudding for a Sunday afternoon at the end of summer, when nobody wants anything heavy and there is still warmth in the garden. It is also good - and this is admittedly slightly less seasonal - in February, served alongside a single shot of cold espresso.

The recipe scales linearly. If you want twice as much, double everything. The freezing time does not change.
item.excerpt:encoded: A two-ingredient recipe with structured ingredient and method lists.

item.title: Shop: Pocket notebook, undated
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
A small pocket notebook, sewn binding, undated, in three cover colors. Made to fit the back pocket of a pair of work trousers without complaining.

What's in it

  • 96 pages of cream uncoated paper, lightly ruled.
  • Sewn binding, lies flat when open.
  • A short list of useful conversions on the inside back cover (oven

temperatures, cup-to-gram, paper sizes), printed in two colors.

Specifications

  • 90 × 140 mm.
  • Cover: 350gsm uncoated card, in terracotta, sage, or deep teal.
  • Paper: 90gsm cream, faintly ruled at 6mm.

Price: $14, or three for $36.

Availability: In stock, all three colors. We restock these every six weeks; they are part of the small line we make in the back of the studio and they are not going anywhere.

Shop for the order form, or pick one up at the front desk during open hours. There is a small wire basket on the counter.
item.excerpt:encoded: A small pocket notebook, sewn binding, 96 pages, undated. Includes price, paper weight, and stocking note.

item.title: The grammar of maps
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

A map is, on the face of it, a picture of a place. This is misleading. A map is an argument about a place. The cartographer chooses what to put in and what to leave out, what to enlarge and what to suppress, what to colour red and what to render in faint grey. The choices are not innocent. They add up to a sentence the map is making about the territory.

The most interesting things about a map are the things it has decided not to show.

Generalisation

The technical term is generalisation. A real coastline, viewed from a satellite, is a fractal mess of inlets and small headlands. A map of that coast at 1:50,000 simplifies it - it draws a smooth curve where there are in fact a thousand small wiggles. At 1:250,000 the curve gets even smoother; many small bays disappear entirely. At 1:1,000,000 the coast becomes a few sweeping lines.

You cannot make a useful map without generalising. The exact shape of the shore changes every time the tide comes in, anyway. But each level of generalisation makes a choice. A particular small cove might still appear on the 1:50,000 map and have vanished on the 1:250,000. The cartographer decided it was important enough at one scale and not important enough at another. Probably nobody asked the people who lived in the cove.

What the colours mean

Open any modern road atlas. The motorways are red or blue, depending on the country. The major roads are yellow or orange. The minor roads are white, or thin black lines, or in some atlases not shown at all. Settlements above a certain population threshold are named in larger type than settlements below it. National borders are heavier than regional borders. Rivers are blue, forests are green, marshland is a kind of stippled green.

These are conventions, learned in childhood, that we no longer notice. They were not inevitable. The Ordnance Survey colours one set of maps in a particular way. Soviet military maps used a different palette and would have looked, to a British navigator of the same era, faintly alien. The map is in a language; you have to learn the language to read it.

What the projection commits to

Any flat map of a round world is a lie, the question is just which lie. Mercator preserves angles, which is useful for sailing in a straight line, but it makes Greenland look the size of Africa. Peters preserves area, which is useful for political arguments, but the shapes are absurd - South America turns into a sausage. The polar stereographic preserves shapes near the pole at the cost of ridiculously enlarging the equator.

There is no neutral projection. Every map of the world has decided which of its lies it is willing to tell. Mercator's shape-preservation made it the default for most of the European colonial era, partly because the same property that made it useful for sailors also made it convenient for empires - it makes the temperate north look bigger and the tropical south look smaller. The convenience and the ideology are not unrelated.

What an honest map omits

The honest map admits its omissions. The little legend in the corner says scale 1:50,000 and contour interval 10m and projection: transverse Mercator. It says, in effect, here is what I am pretending to be a picture of, and here is roughly how much I have flattened, smoothed, recoloured, and dropped. A map without a scale bar, without a north arrow, without a projection note, is hiding things. Sometimes it is hiding things on purpose.

The map you draw on the back of a napkin to explain to a friend how to find your house is also hiding things. It does not bother with a scale bar because it does not need one - both you and your friend know it is not to scale. It does not bother with a north arrow because, again, you both know which way north is. It is honest in the way a child's drawing is honest: it shows the things that matter and ignores the things that do not. There are worse models for cartography.
item.excerpt:encoded: How cartographers compose information — and what an honest map omits.

item.title: Closing early Thursday
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
A short note: we are closing at three on Thursday, the 15th, so the kitchen can do a deep clean and the print room can do an inventory. The shop is open in the morning as usual, until three. Sorry about the early close.

We will be open at the regular time on Friday — ten until six.
item.excerpt:encoded: A two-paragraph aside about an early closing for studio cleaning.

item.title: Twelve fountain pens
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

Twelve pens, photographed at a small north-facing window over the course of a quiet afternoon. None of them is mine. All of them belong to a friend whose name I have asked to keep out of the writeup.

[gallery columns="3" size="medium" link="file"]

The first pen, a steel-nibbed school pen of the kind you can still buy in any stationer for the price of a cup of coffee. The nib is fine. The grip is plastic. It writes a thin, slightly scratchy line, and the ink dries on the page within a few seconds. It is the cheapest pen in the room and one of the most pleasant to use.

The second is a much older pen, a piston-filler from the 1950s with a gold nib. The nib has been sprung at some point in its history and now writes with a soft give that makes every letter feel as though it is being placed deliberately. The ink dries slowly. The line varies with the angle.

The third pen has not written in a long time. The ink in the converter has dried into a small block of pigment. We will leave it where it is.

The fourth, fifth and sixth pens are all the same model - a modern Japanese pen sold in three colours, with three matching converters and three matching boxes. My friend bought all three on a single afternoon, in a single shop, in a city that has very good pen shops. None of them has ever leaked. The nibs are interchangeable.

The seventh pen is a calligraphy dip pen, which is not strictly a fountain pen at all. We are including it because the holder is beautiful.

The eighth pen is plastic and forgettable.

The ninth, tenth and eleventh pens are all variants on a single design - a cigar-shaped resin barrel with a steel-and-gold two-tone nib - in three colours that the manufacturer no longer makes. They write almost identically. The colour is the only meaningful difference between them.

The twelfth pen is the one my friend uses every day. It is a forty-year-old pen with a slightly bent nib, a barrel that has been polished smooth by decades of being in and out of pockets, and a converter that needs to be refilled approximately once a fortnight. It writes a steady, medium line in a colour my friend mixes themselves from two bottles of commercial ink. Asked to choose one pen out of the twelve to keep, my friend would not hesitate.

This is, on balance, how it usually goes. You can have any number of pens. You will use one of them.
item.excerpt:encoded: An illustrated catalogue of twelve pens, with captions on each.

item.title: Harbor light at five o'clock
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

The light in a small harbor at five o'clock in the afternoon, at this time of year, is approximately the colour of the inside of a brass instrument. It is not yellow. It is not orange. It is the particular metallic colour that brass takes on when somebody has not polished it for a long time but also has not let it tarnish.

The light comes in low across the water and turns every white surface - the white of a sail, the white of a railing, the white of a sleeping seagull - into the same brass colour. The hulls of the moored boats, which are mostly painted some shade of cream or ivory, also become brass. The water itself takes on the colour of the harbor floor, which here is sand, which means the water becomes a brass-coloured mirror with the sailboats reflected in it.

At about ten past five, the harbor light at the end of the breakwater comes on. It is a small, white, unimpressive light, much less interesting than a real lighthouse, but for the next twenty minutes it is competing with the sunset, and it loses. By half past five the sun is below the horizon, the brass colour drains out of everything, and the harbor light suddenly becomes the brightest thing in the scene. The boats turn into silhouettes. The water goes black.

Five minutes after that the second light comes on - a red light, on the other side of the harbor entrance - and the harbor is once again, in the old language, "open." The boats can come in.

I am sitting on a bench. I have been sitting on this bench for about forty-five minutes. None of the boats have come in.

This is, in my experience, often how it goes.
item.excerpt:encoded: A short photo-essay about evening light on a small harbor.

item.title: A tea blend for cold mornings
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
We have started serving a new house tea blend in the kitchen, on a trial basis through the end of January. If it does not catch on, it will quietly go away. So far it is catching on.

It is a black-tea base — a Ceylon and a small amount of an Assam, both loose-leaf — with cinnamon Tomás toasts in the small skillet at the stove, a few crushed cardamom pods, and a single allspice berry per pot. It is sweet enough that you will not need sugar. It is meant for cold mornings.

The name we are using on the Menu is "House blend, winter," which is wordy, but every other name we tried turned out to be a name a tea company already uses and has presumably already trademarked.

If you would like to take some home, there is a small tin in the Shop — see the House tea, in a small tin post for what is in it and how it brews.
item.excerpt:encoded: A note on the new house tea blend — what's in it, who toasts the cinnamon, and where to buy a tin.

item.title: Shop: House tea, in a small tin
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
A small tin of the kitchen's winter house blend, blended in the back of the studio. Enough for about thirty pots if you are generous with the leaves and forty if you are not.

What's in it

  • Ceylon black tea (single estate).
  • A small amount of Assam, for body.
  • Cinnamon, lightly toasted before blending.
  • Crushed cardamom pods.
  • Whole allspice.

That is everything. No flavorings, no oils, no sweeteners, no decoration. Pleasant tea.

Specifications

  • 100 grams loose-leaf, in a sealed steel tin.
  • Tin is reusable; please bring it back when it is empty and we will refill

it for half the price.

Price: $22, or $11 for a refill in your own tin.

Availability: In stock through end of February. We blend in small batches; if it sells through faster than expected we may pause and resume.

To brew: A heaped teaspoon per cup, water just off the boil, four minutes. No milk, in our opinion. Sugar is up to you.

Shop for the order form, or pick one up at the front desk.
item.excerpt:encoded: A small tin of the house blend. Includes price, weight, and steeping notes.

item.title: An essay elsewhere, worth reading
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
A short link out: The grain of the page by a friend of the studio, on the surprisingly long history of laid versus wove paper and why it matters more than you would expect for the way a book feels in the hand.

It is the kind of essay we wish we had written ourselves and are glad someone else did. Recommended for anyone who has wondered why some books feel right and others do not.
item.excerpt:encoded: A link-format Dispatch entry pointing to a single outbound URL.

item.title: On quiet rooms
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

A quiet room is not a silent room. A silent room is unsettling - you can hear your own pulse, and the small sounds of the building cooling and warming, and the faint ringing in your ears that is always there but usually masked. A quiet room has a low, steady, ignorable hum: a refrigerator three rooms away, a fan in the next building, a ventilator on the roof. The hum is what makes the room feel safe. The silence is what makes it feel haunted.

A note I keep coming back to. I think about it whenever I am in a hotel room with the air conditioning off.
item.excerpt:encoded: A quote-format Dispatch built around a single short pull-quote.

item.title: Things found in a library window
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

The library on the corner has one tall window that catches the afternoon sun, and on the wide sill of that window the librarians have, over many decades, built up a small museum.

Today, on the sill, in roughly the order I notice them:

  • A glass paperweight, about the size of a small apple, with a single

pressed flower inside it. The flower is a daisy, mostly white, with the yellow centre faded to a kind of soft cream.

  • Three glass marbles, very ordinary, of the kind a child would carry in

a pocket.

  • A small bronze figurine of a tortoise, about three centimetres long,

worn smooth by being picked up.

  • A library card belonging to a previous reader, folded in quarters and

used as a bookmark in a book that has long since been returned without it. The card lists thirty-eight checkouts. The dates are from the late 1970s.

  • A letter opener with a horn handle, blunted by use.
  • A small ceramic dish containing five paperclips, two safety pins, a

rubber band, and a single bus ticket from another country.

These things are not displayed. They are not catalogued. The librarians have, between them, agreed to let them accumulate. Once or twice a year somebody dusts the sill, picks up the items individually, looks at each one for a moment, and puts it back roughly where it was. Nothing has ever been removed.

I have been coming to this library for nine years. In that time, two new things have been added: the bus ticket and the bronze tortoise. Both appeared on the same day, about three years ago, with no announcement and no explanation. I have never asked who put them there. It would feel slightly impolite.

The light comes in across the sill at a low angle for about an hour each afternoon. For that hour the paperweight catches the sun and the daisy inside it glows. I usually try, on days when I am at the library at the right time, to be sitting at the desk that has the best view of it.
item.excerpt:encoded: A list essay catalogued by what the afternoon sun illuminates.

item.title: Notes from a small observatory
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

The observatory is on a hilltop about forty minutes' drive from the nearest town, on a piece of land that was donated to a local astronomy society in the 1970s by a farmer who, depending on which version of the story you believe, either believed in their work or just did not want the land any more.

The dome is small. The telescope inside it is small. The members of the society, on the night I visited, numbered four. They had set out three folding chairs at the back of the dome, a flask of tea, and a plate of biscuits.

The thing being observed, that night, was Saturn. The rings were sharp. The moons - I counted four, though there were probably more present - sat in a casual diagonal across the field of view. The other members took turns at the eyepiece. Each of them murmured "still there" or "still beautiful" or, in one case, "still impossible," and then stepped back to let the next person look.

I think this is what I want to record about the visit. There was no discovery being made. There was no programme of observations. There was no data being collected. Four people had driven up a hill, on a Tuesday evening, in indifferent weather, to look at a planet that they had all looked at many times before, that has not changed in any human sense in hundreds of millions of years, and that will be exactly the same the next time they look at it. They were, in the most ordinary sense, paying attention.

I have been thinking about this for several months. I do not have anything clever to add. The biscuits, in case you were wondering, were shortbread.
item.excerpt:encoded: A recap-style post about a quiet evening at a small hilltop observatory.

item.title: Two photographs of snow
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
A short Kitchen note on cooking in cold weather. The first snowdrop of the year, photographed in the back garden at half past eight on a Sunday morning. The snow is the second snow of the season; the first snow vanished in a day.

We are using the cold snap as an excuse to bring back the winter house blend on the Menu and to put the slow-roasted chicken back on for Friday. Cold weather makes you want to cook things that take a long time.
item.excerpt:encoded: Image-format Kitchen post — two photographs and a sentence about cooking in cold weather.

item.title: A six-second clip from the print room
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

[video src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nickhamze/demo-dispatch/main/images/_media/paper-boat-clip.mp4" poster="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nickhamze/demo-dispatch/main/images/paper-boat/paper-boat--16x9-1200.webp" width="1280" height="720"][/video]

A folded paper boat in a shallow puddle, six seconds long, no sound. Filmed on a phone in a courtyard during a brief gap in the rain. The boat rotates about a quarter-turn over the course of the clip and then the wind catches it and it leaves the frame.
item.excerpt:encoded: Video-format Dispatch post that embeds the bundled MP4.

item.title: Folding a paper boat
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

You will need one rectangular sheet of paper. A4, US Letter, or any proportion close to root-two will work; the boat is more elegant on lighter paper, but heavier paper floats longer.

Steps

  1. Fold the paper in half along the short axis. Crease the fold firmly

with a thumbnail. The folded edge is now at the top.

  1. Fold the top corners down to meet at the centre line. You should

end up with a triangular shape on top of an unfolded strip at the bottom.

  1. **Fold the unfolded bottom strip up on the front, then turn the model

over and fold the matching strip up on the back.** The model is now a triangle with a flat base and two folded flaps.

  1. Open out the base and reshape the model into a square. Bring the

two pointed corners together at the bottom; the model collapses into a diamond.

  1. **Fold the bottom point of the diamond up on the front and on the

back.** You now have a smaller triangle. Open out the base again and collapse it into a smaller diamond, then pull the two top corners apart to form the boat.

The boat will float for somewhere between thirty seconds and three minutes, depending on the paper and the water. It is improved, in my view, by being launched with a small ceremony - a hand on the side of the puddle, a short word, an unhurried release.
item.excerpt:encoded: Five numbered steps with one inline image per step.

item.title: On a particular teacup
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

I have been drinking out of the same teacup, more or less every morning, for eleven years. It was given to me by an aunt who is no longer here. It is white porcelain, very thin, with a small hairline crack running from the rim about a third of the way down one side. It came with a saucer that I no longer have - it broke in the third year of ownership - and a lid that I have never used.

This is, in effect, a review of one specific teacup. Your teacup is not this teacup. Your aunt was not my aunt. Take the rating below with that understanding.

At a glance

| Property | Value | | --------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | | Material | Bone china, very thin | | Capacity | Approximately 180ml | | Heat retention | Poor (this is, in this case, a feature) | | Lip comfort | Excellent | | Dishwasher safe | The label says yes; I would not risk it | | Replaceable | No | | Score | Five stars out of five |

The poor heat retention means the tea cools to drinking temperature within about three minutes. This means I drink the cup of tea I have made, when I have made it, rather than coming back to it half an hour later to find it cold and undrinkable. The thinness of the porcelain means the cup itself feels almost weightless. The hairline crack does not leak. The absence of a saucer is a small daily inconvenience that I have stopped noticing.

I will not buy another teacup. When this teacup eventually breaks - and I expect it will, in some careless morning, in my hand or somebody else's

  • I will probably not replace it at all. There are enough other cups in

the house. None of them is this cup.
item.excerpt:encoded: A short essay about a single ceramic teacup, with a star-rating-shaped table at the end.

item.title: Episode 1: Paper
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

[audio src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nickhamze/demo-dispatch/main/images/_media/episode-1-paper.mp3" preload="metadata"][/audio]

The first episode of an occasional podcast about small things. Mira and I sit at a kitchen table for forty-three minutes and talk about paper: the surface, the sound, the smell, the way it ages, and why a particular A4 sheet from a particular Italian mill has been sitting in a folder on Mira's desk for nine years without ever being written on.

Chapters

  1. (00:00) Cold open: rain on a tin roof.
  2. (01:14) Introductions and the paper on the desk.
  3. (06:32) Why the surface of a paper is the most important thing about

it, and what "tooth" means.

  1. (13:48) The smell of paper, and why old paper smells different from

new paper. (Hint: the lignin.)

  1. (21:15) A short tangent about the sound of paper, and why some

papers are noisier than others.

  1. (28:40) The Italian mill, and the nine-year-old A4 sheet.
  2. (36:02) Paper sizes and the square root of two.
  3. (40:55) Closing thoughts and the next episode.

A transcript will follow in a week or two.
item.excerpt:encoded: A self-hosted MP3 episode with a chapter list rendered as an OL.

item.title: An evening on the pier
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

The pier is short - perhaps two hundred metres, end to end - and on most weekday evenings there is nobody on it but a handful of fishermen and one or two people walking dogs. The fishermen do not, in my observation, catch much. The dogs are interested in the smells and not in the water.

I came down to watch the boats come in. There were three boats in the bay when I arrived, sails up, tacking towards the harbour entrance in the kind of slow, thoughtful zigzag that a sailing boat makes when the wind is light and behind it. The lead boat made the entrance at about half past seven. The second boat followed about ten minutes later. The third boat gave up, dropped anchor, and decided to wait until morning.

I have been thinking about the third boat ever since. It is a hard thing to do, knowing the harbour is right there, and to choose instead to spend the night on the open water. There must be reasons. None of them are visible from a pier.

By eight o'clock the harbour was full and the light had drained out of the sky. I walked back along the pier in the half-dark. The fishermen had packed up. The dogs had gone home.

It was, all in all, a successful evening.
item.excerpt:encoded: A short essay about boats coming in.

item.title: A kettle and a clock
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

Our kettle takes exactly four minutes and eleven seconds to boil from cold. I know this because I have, over many mornings, timed it. The kettle does not know it. The kettle is a perfectly ordinary stovetop kettle, in our case enamel, with a small whistle in the spout that makes a noise of about two seconds' duration once the water is at a full rolling boil.

This means our kitchen has, every morning, a soft alarm clock that goes off four minutes and eleven seconds after we turn the gas on. It is the sound, more than any other, that wakes the household. It is also, by some distance, the most reliable timekeeper in the house. Our actual clocks all disagree with each other by a minute or two. The kettle agrees with the sun.

I find this enormously comforting. The kettle does not know what time it is. It does not know what day it is. It does not know whether anybody is awake to hear it. It just boils for four minutes and eleven seconds, every morning, and announces that it has finished.

It is a small thing, and it has been the same small thing for the eleven years we have lived in this kitchen, and it will probably continue to be the same small thing for whatever time we have left here. The kettle is older than my marriage, older than our cat, older than the table the kettle sits on top of. It has not changed. It will not change.

I am not making a larger point. I am only saying that the kettle is, in its particular way, a clock.
item.excerpt:encoded: An essay about the sound of a kettle as a domestic timekeeper.

item.title: Stones that fit the hand
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

There is a particular kind of stone, found on most river banks in most parts of the world, that fits exactly into a closed adult hand. It is heavier than you expect for its size. It is smooth in a way that no manufactured object is smooth - smoothed not by sandpaper but by tens of thousands of years of being moved very slowly, by water, against other similar stones. It is usually a quiet colour: grey, or pale brown, or a muted green.

I have a small collection of these stones on a shelf in my kitchen. None of them was acquired with intent. Each was picked up on a walk, carried in a pocket for the rest of the day, and set down on the shelf in the evening because I could not think of a reason to throw it away. There are now, according to a quick count, eleven of them.

The first time I noticed I was doing this was about six years ago. The collection at that point was four stones, on the windowsill of a kitchen in another flat. Moving house, I packed them carefully, one at a time, in a small cardboard box. The movers did not ask. The stones travelled across town in the cab of the van, on the dashboard, and were set out on the new windowsill before any of the books were unpacked.

I am not sure why I keep them. They are, honestly, just stones. They will outlast everything else in the kitchen, including the kitchen, and one day somebody will find them and not know whose they were or why they were on a shelf, and they will throw the stones away or, if they are kind, take them out to a river and put them back.

It will, in either case, be the right thing to do.
item.excerpt:encoded: A meditative essay about river stones.

item.title: The fern uncurls slowly
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

There is a fern on the kitchen windowsill that produces, every spring, a single new frond. The frond emerges, in late February or early March, as a small tight spiral - what botanists call a fiddlehead, after the curled top of a violin - and over the course of about six weeks it slowly uncurls into a flat, feathered green leaf that is roughly the size of my hand.

The uncurling is, on any given day, invisible. You can sit and watch the fiddlehead for an hour and nothing will happen. But if you take a photograph of it on Monday and another photograph on Friday, the difference is unmistakable. The spiral has loosened. The pale green has darkened. The whole structure has rotated slightly to face the window.

This is the seventh year I have lived with this fern. Each year I tell myself I am going to take a photograph every day of the uncurling, end to end, and assemble them into a small flipbook. Each year I forget after about the third day. The fern continues to uncurl regardless.

This year, again, I am going to take a photograph every day. We will see how it goes.
item.excerpt:encoded: Essay tracking a single fern across a season.

item.title: Bicycles I have known
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews.

Three bicycles, in chronological order, with brief notes on each.

The first bicycle

A second-hand mixte frame, possibly French, possibly Italian, with five gears and a rod brake on the front. It was painted, when I bought it, a particular dark green that the previous owner had applied with what looked like a household paintbrush. The brushstrokes were visible. I liked them.

It was stolen, four months after I bought it, from outside a library where I had locked it carefully to a railing that was, it turned out, not attached to anything. The railing went too. I have always slightly hoped that the thief took the railing with them.

The second bicycle

A pragmatic, slightly ugly steel city bike, painted black, with a basket on the front and a rack on the back. I owned it for nine years. It went to work. It went to the supermarket. It went to the river. Once, fully loaded, it carried two weeks' worth of holiday luggage to a campsite an hour away and then carried us both home again. The frame was indestructible. The components were not.

I gave it away when I moved cities. The friend I gave it to is, as far as I know, still riding it.

The third bicycle

A folding bike, partly because the new flat has no bike storage and partly because I have always slightly fancied a folding bike. It folds in about ten seconds. It rides surprisingly well. The wheels are small enough that the ride is bumpy on cobbles, which fortunately the new city has very few of.

It does not have a name. The previous two had names. I am not sure why this one does not. Perhaps it will earn one in time.
item.excerpt:encoded: An affectionate list essay about three former bicycles.

item.title: Fluid typography stress
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews. (Sandbox post.)

Heading level one

Heading level two

Heading level three

Heading level four

Heading level five
Heading level six

A paragraph at the body size. This paragraph contains the kind of normal prose you would actually expect to read in a long-form article and is intentionally long enough that the active theme has to demonstrate how it handles ordinary measure - which is to say, line length - across the width of the content area.

A short paragraph.

A paragraph that contains the longest single token in the dataset: Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän. The word is German. It refers, roughly, to a captain of a Danube steamship company, and it is the word the dataset uses to verify that the active theme breaks long words appropriately at narrow viewports without overflowing the content area or producing horizontal scroll.

A paragraph at the body size, again, included so that the previous paragraph has a normal-prose neighbour to contrast with.
item.excerpt:encoded: One paragraph at every heading level, one extremely short paragraph, and one paragraph containing the longest word in the dataset.

item.title: Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews. (Sandbox post.)

The post title and the body both contain the longest single token in the dataset: Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän. The token is meant to verify that the active theme breaks long words at narrow viewports.

Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän, again, in a paragraph by itself.

Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän, Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän, Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän, in a paragraph as a list of three repetitions separated by commas, to verify that line breaking still works when the long word appears multiple times in close proximity.
item.excerpt:encoded: Title and body both contain the longest word in the dataset.

item.title: Many comments — pagination test
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
This is sample content for theme previews. (Sandbox post.)

A short post that hosts 53 threaded comments to exercise comment pagination at the WordPress default of five comments per page. The post body is intentionally brief - the interesting thing on this page is the comment list below.

The comments are auto-generated by scripts/build-wxr.js from the template in content/comments/many-comments.yaml and include at least one comment of every variant the spec requires: a code snippet, a long URL, an emoji-only reply, an @mention (as plain text), an inline image, a comment by the post author, a multilingual reply, a pingback, a trackback, and one comment in pending-moderation status.
item.excerpt:encoded: A short post that hosts 53 threaded comments to exercise comment pagination at the WP default of 5 per page.

item.title: 光と紙
item.dc:creator: akosua
item.content:encoded:
このコンテンツはテーマプレビュー用のサンプルです。

光は紙の上をゆっくりと移動し、午前の早い時間に窓辺の机に届きます。机の上には一冊のノートと、一本の万年筆と、半分まで満たされたインク瓶があります。それ以外のものはありません。

紙は古いものではありません。先週、町の文房具店で買ったばかりです。表面はわずかに粗く、ペン先がほどよく引っかかります。書くことは、まだ何も決まっていません。

この投稿は、テーマが日本語のフォントとレイアウトをどのように扱うかを確認するためのものです。
item.excerpt:encoded: Short Japanese-language post titled "Light and Paper".

item.title: الضوء والورق
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
هذا محتوى نموذجي لمعاينات القوالب.

الضوء يتحرك ببطء عبر سطح الورقة في الصباح الباكر، ويصل إلى المكتب الذي يقع بجانب النافذة. على المكتب دفتر واحد، وقلم حبر، وزجاجة حبر مملوءة إلى المنتصف. لا شيء آخر.

الورق ليس قديماً. اشتُري الأسبوع الماضي من متجر القرطاسية في البلدة. السطح خشن قليلاً، والقلم يتحرك عليه بسلاسة معقولة. لم يُقرَّر بعد ما سيُكتب.

هذه التدوينة موجودة للتحقق من كيفية تعامل القالب النشط مع النصوص العربية واتجاه الكتابة من اليمين إلى اليسار.
item.excerpt:encoded: Short Arabic-language post (right-to-left).

item.title: प्रकाश और कागज़
item.dc:creator: tomas
item.content:encoded:
यह सामग्री थीम पूर्वावलोकन के लिए नमूना है।

सुबह की रोशनी धीरे-धीरे कागज़ की सतह पर फैलती है और खिड़की के पास रखी मेज़ तक पहुँचती है। मेज़ पर एक नोटबुक है, एक फाउंटेन पेन है, और आधी भरी हुई स्याही की एक बोतल है। इनके अलावा और कुछ नहीं।

कागज़ पुराना नहीं है। पिछले हफ़्ते शहर की एक छोटी दुकान से खरीदा गया था। सतह थोड़ी खुरदरी है, और कलम उस पर सहजता से चलती है। अभी तक यह तय नहीं हुआ है कि उस पर क्या लिखा जाएगा।

यह पोस्ट सक्रिय थीम के साथ देवनागरी लिपि के व्यवहार की जाँच के लिए है।
item.excerpt:encoded: Short Hindi-language post titled "Light and Paper".

item.title: Свет и бумага
item.dc:creator: mira
item.content:encoded:
Это образец содержимого для предварительного просмотра темы.

Утренний свет медленно движется по поверхности листа бумаги и достигает письменного стола у окна. На столе одна тетрадь, одна перьевая ручка и наполовину наполненная чернильница. Больше ничего.

Бумага не старая. Куплена на прошлой неделе в небольшом магазине канцелярских товаров. Поверхность слегка шероховатая, перо движется по ней с приятным сопротивлением. Что именно будет на ней написано, ещё не решено.

Эта запись существует для проверки того, как активная тема обрабатывает кириллический шрифт.
item.excerpt:encoded: Short Russian-language post titled "Light and Paper".

item.title: Φως και χαρτί
item.dc:creator: daniyal
item.content:encoded:
Αυτό είναι δείγμα περιεχομένου για την προεπισκόπηση θεμάτων.

Το πρωινό φως κινείται αργά πάνω στην επιφάνεια του χαρτιού και φτάνει στο τραπέζι κοντά στο παράθυρο. Πάνω στο τραπέζι υπάρχει ένα τετράδιο, ένα στυλό με πένα και ένα μελανοδοχείο γεμάτο μέχρι τη μέση. Τίποτε άλλο.

Το χαρτί δεν είναι παλιό. Αγοράστηκε την προηγούμενη εβδομάδα από ένα μικρό χαρτοπωλείο της πόλης. Η επιφάνεια είναι ελαφρώς τραχιά και η πένα γλιστρά πάνω της με ευχάριστη αντίσταση. Τι ακριβώς θα γραφτεί δεν έχει αποφασιστεί ακόμη.

Αυτή η ανάρτηση υπάρχει για να δοκιμάσει πώς το ενεργό θέμα χειρίζεται την ελληνική γραφή.
item.excerpt:encoded: Short Greek-language post titled "Light and Paper".

item.title: ☕ A morning
